rawContent:
Tweet by @mfpiccolo (Mike Piccolo)
Author bio: Founder of @iiidevs Co-founder and board member of https://t.co/imPpSoV4Gu
Text: Most AI agent stack diagrams treat the harness as a special layer.

It sits between the model, memory, tools, orchestration, observability, and the rest of the backend. That looks clean in a diagram. In practice every connection becomes integration code. Adapters. SDKs. Queues. State. Retries. Permissions. Logging. Tracing. Deployment. Versioning.

The difference with iii is not that the harness is a better layer.

The harness is not a layer.

On iii, the harness is workers. Context management, session state, LLM routing, tool discovery, agent loops, sandboxing, observability. Each one is a worker. They use the same model as the rest of the system.

A queue is a worker. An API is a worker. A cron scheduler is a worker. An agent is a worker. A browser can be a worker. A Raspberry Pi can be a worker. Your application code can be a worker.

Anything that connects to iii exposes its capabilities to the rest of the system in real time. Workers expose functions. Triggers activate functions. The engine routes between them.

Adding a capability is not adding another service and integrating it with every other layer. It is adding a worker whose functions are discoverable and callable by the system.

The diagram on the left treats the agent harness as the center of a separate AI stack.
The diagram on the right treats the harness as a composition of workers inside the same execution system as everything else.
